persons.arguments_against: The strongest counter: there are 3 Ransleys in the Malta staff lists, so in principle a same-name relative or a successor could hold the Director of Prisons post, and the bio carries no honour to cross-check (honour FAIL). This fails because the evidence is multi-dimensional and the office is singular: both initials G.J. match, the bio's career culminates exactly at 'dir., prisons, 1944', and the stint shows 'G. J. Ransley, Director of Prisons' held continuously 1949-1960 -- one man holds Director of Prisons at a time, and the bio's 1944 appointment flows directly into the stint with no gap. Age (b.1900, age 49 in 1949) fits, and there are 3 agreeing edition-years with position similarity 78. A second G. J. Ransley simultaneously or successively directing Malta's prisons is implausible.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence that the Director of Prisons post passed to a different G. J. Ransley between 1944 and 1949 would reverse this.

notes: Two-initial match, singular department-head post matching the bio's terminal appointment, chronological continuity from 1944, consistent age, 3 agreeing edition-years. High-confidence match.
